headline: Bumiputera Corporate Equity Rule Forces 42% of Startups to Add Partners
context: MDEC data shows 42% of non-Bumiputera tech startups added Bumiputera partners solely to meet equity requirements for government contracts and incentive programmes in 2024.

cards.big: 42% of non-Bumiputera tech startups added partners solely for equity compliance.
cards.sub: The practice — called 'Ali Baba' — is illegal, widespread, and structurally incentivised.

cards.big: Government incentive programmes require minimum 30% Bumiputera equity.
cards.sub: For startups, this means diluting founder ownership to qualify for grants worth RM50,000-500,000.

cards.big: MACC investigated 23 Ali Baba arrangement cases in 2024.
cards.sub: Zero convictions resulted. The practice is too systemic to prosecute case-by-case.

cards.big: Singapore has no ethnic equity requirement for startup incentives.
cards.sub: Malaysian startups relocating to Singapore rose 34% between 2022 and 2024.

cards.big: A rule that forces 42% of startups to create sham partnerships is not building equity. It is building deception.
Startup ecosystems run on talent and speed. Equity requirements that incentivise fake partnerships over real capability are driving the entrepreneurs Malaysia needs most to leave.
